blocks.body: Present Simple: Negatives — do not, does not, don't, doesn't, and the base form of the verb.

{"level":"A1–A2","intro":"In Present Simple negatives, we put do not / does not before the main verb.","rules":[{"label":"I / You / We / They don't","color":"emerald","text":"Use do not or don't with these subjects.","example":"I don't work on Sundays."},{"label":"He / She / It doesn't","color":"blue","text":"Use does not or doesn't with he / she / it.","example":"She doesn't like coffee."},{"label":"Base verb after do / does","color":"amber","text":"After do / does, the verb goes back to the base form.","example":"He doesn't go, not he doesn't goes."}]}

{"title":"1. Negative formula","intro":"Remember the two main patterns.","items":[{"label":"I / You / We / They","title":"do not + V1","subtitle":"I do not work here. / We don't live near the station."},{"label":"He / She / It","title":"does not + V1","subtitle":"He does not play tennis. / She doesn't drive."},{"label":"Full forms","title":"do not / does not","subtitle":"The full forms sound a little more formal or more emphatic."},{"label":"Short forms","title":"don't / doesn't","subtitle":"In everyday English, short forms are very common."}]}

{"title":"2. How it works in a sentence","sections":[{"label":"Habits","color":"emerald","description":"Say what you do not usually do.","examples":[{"en":"I don't drink coffee at night.","ua":"I don't drink coffee at night."},{"en":"They don't walk to school.","ua":"They don't walk to school."}]},{"label":"He / She / It","color":"blue","description":"With third person singular, use doesn't.","examples":[{"en":"He doesn't watch TV in the morning.","ua":"He doesn't watch TV in the morning."},{"en":"My sister doesn't eat meat.","ua":"My sister doesn't eat meat."}]},{"label":"Positive vs negative","color":"amber","description":"Compare the affirmative and the negative: in the negative, the verb stays in V1.","examples":[{"en":"She works here. → She doesn't work here.","ua":"After doesn't, works becomes work."},{"en":"We play on Fridays. → We don't play on Fridays.","ua":"After don't, the verb stays in the base form."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Affirmative and negative","intro":"In the negative, add do / does + not.","rows":[{"en":"I work on Sundays.","ua":"I don't work on Sundays.","note":"do + not + base verb"},{"en":"They like spicy food.","ua":"They don't like spicy food.","note":"don't + like"},{"en":"He plays tennis.","ua":"He doesn't play tennis.","note":"doesn't + play"},{"en":"She goes by bus.","ua":"She doesn't go by bus.","note":"doesn't + go"}],"warning":"Do not say He doesn't goes."}

{"title":"4. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using don't with he / she / it.","wrong":"❌ He don't like tea.","right":"✅ He doesn't like tea."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Keeping -s after doesn't.","wrong":"❌ She doesn't goes to school by bus.","right":"✅ She doesn't go to school by bus."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using not without do.","wrong":"❌ I not work on Sundays.","right":"✅ I don't work on Sundays."}]}

{"title":"5. Short summary","items":["A Present Simple negative = do / does + not + V1.","Use don't or do not with I / you / we / they.","Use doesn't or does not with he / she / it.","After do / does, always use the base form of the verb.","A common mistake is doesn't goes instead of doesn't go."]}

{"title":"Present Simple lessons","items":[{"label":"Forms and Use","url":"/en/theory/present-simple/present-simple-forms","current":false},{"label":"Negatives","url":"/en/theory/present-simple/present-simple-negatives","current":true},{"label":"Questions and Short Answers","url":"/en/theory/present-simple/present-simple-questions","current":false},{"label":"Adverbs of Frequency","url":"/en/theory/present-simple/present-simple-adverbs-of-frequency","current":false}]}
